## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Light Sensor Choices for IoT

| Parameter | **LDR (CdS Cell)** | **Photodiode (BPW34)** | **Phototransistor (TEMT6000)** | **Digital IC (BH1750)** | **Digital IC (TSL2561)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output Type | Analog (resistive) | Analog (current) | Analog (current) | Digital (I²C) | Digital (I²C) |
| Spectral Response | Visible (550 nm peak) | Visible + IR (wide) | Visible (570 nm peak) | Visible (matched to eye) | Visible + IR (rejected) |
| Lux Range | ~1 to ~10,000 | ~1 to ~100,000 | ~1 to ~10,000 | ~1 to 65,535 | ~0.1 to 40,000 |
| Linearity | Non-linear (logarithmic) | Linear (with TIA) | Quasi-linear | Linear (built-in) | Linear (built-in) |
| Interface Complexity | Very Low (1 resistor) | Low (op-amp needed) | Low (resistor) | Medium (I²C driver) | Medium (I²C driver) |
| Cost (approx.) | ₹5–₹15 | ₹25–₹50 | ₹40–₹80 | ₹120–₹250 | ₹180–₹350 |
| IoT Suitability | Beginner / educational | Precision analog | Quick prototyping | **Best for production IoT** | **Best for smartphone-grade** |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — ADC Resolution Calculation

**Problem:** An ESP32 microcontroller uses a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V. What is the smallest voltage change it can resolve?

### Step 1 — Identify Bit Depth

$$n = 12 \implies 2^n = 4096 \text{ levels}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Resolution Formula

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{4096}$$

$$\Delta V \approx 0.0008057 \text{ V} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV}$$

**Logic:** This means the ESP32 can distinguish voltage steps as small as 0.806 mV — vastly finer than the Arduino Uno's $\frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88$ mV step. For low-light precision sensing, the higher bit-depth ADC is critical.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Inverse-Square Lux Calculation

**Problem:** A streetlamp emits a luminous intensity of 2500 cd in its downward cone. What is the illuminance on the road surface 8 meters directly below?

### Step 1 — Apply the Inverse-Square Law

$$E_v = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$E_v = \frac{2500}{8^2} = \frac{2500}{64}$$

$$E_v \approx 39.06 \text{ lux}$$

**Logic:** This is below the **recommended minimum 50 lux** for urban road lighting per Indian Roads Congress (IRC) standards — suggesting the lamp needs a stronger bulb or closer mounting height.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ambient Light Sensor IoT Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PER["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        LDR1["LDR / Photodiode\nPhotosensitive Element"]
        DIV["Voltage Divider\nR_fixed + R_LDR"]
    end

    subgraph ADC["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
        AMP["Analog Signal\nV_out = 0 to V_ref"]
        QNT["ADC Quantization\n10-bit / 12-bit"]
    end

    subgraph MCU["PROCESSING UNIT"]
        CPU["Microcontroller\nArduino / ESP32"]
        MAP["Calibration Mapping\nf(D) -> Lux"]
        THR["Threshold Logic\nif L less than T_relay"]
    end

    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"]
        TX["Wireless Tx\nWi-Fi / BLE / LoRa"]
        CLD["Cloud Endpoint\nMQTT / HTTP"]
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        UI["Mobile Dashboard\nBlynk / ThingsBoard"]
        ACT["Actuator Trigger\nRelay / MOSFET / LED"]
    end

    LDR1 --> DIV
    DIV --> AMP
    AMP --> QNT
    QNT --> CPU
    CPU --> MAP
    MAP --> THR
    THR --> TX
    TX --> CLD
    CLD --> UI
    THR --> ACT
```

---

## 4.2 Hardware Wiring Schematic — LDR + Arduino Uno

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V5["Arduino +5V pin"]
    GND["Arduino GND pin"]
    A0["Arduino Analog Pin A0"]

    LDRNODE["LDR\nTop terminal"]
    RFIX["R_fixed = 10 kOhm\nBottom terminal"]

    V5 --> LDRNODE
    LDRNODE --> A0
    A0 --> RFIX
    RFIX --> GND
```

**Wiring Logic:**
- The **LDR** is connected between **+5V** and the **analog input A0**.
- The **fixed 10 kΩ resistor** connects **A0** to **GND**, forming the lower half of the voltage divider.
- The junction at **A0** is the analog tap point read by the ADC.
- In **bright light**, $R_{LDR}$ shrinks → voltage at A0 **rises** → ADC count rises.
- In **darkness**, $R_{LDR}$ grows (megaohms) → voltage at A0 **collapses toward 0V** → ADC count falls.

---

## 4.3 Signal Flow Sequence — From Photon to Lux Display

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PHO as Photon Source
    participant LDR as LDR Sensor
    participant DIV as Voltage Divider
    participant ADC as ADC Channel
    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant DISP as Dashboard

    PHO->>LDR: Incident light (lux L)
    LDR->>LDR: R_LDR changes inversely
    LDR->>DIV: Forms variable top resistor
    DIV->>ADC: V_out proportional to L
    ADC->>MCU: Digital count D (0 to 1023)
    MCU->>MCU: Apply empirical formula
    MCU->>MCU: Compute L = f(D)
    MCU->>DISP: Push L value via Wi-Fi
    DISP-->>MCU: ACK confirmation
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Light Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Need to measure ambient light?"]
    S0 -->|Yes| S1{"Budget tight?\nless than Rs 20"}
    S0 -->|No| SKIP["Not applicable"]

    S1 -->|Yes| LDRP["Use LDR CdS Cell\nAnalog voltage divider"]
    S1 -->|No| S2{"Need lux accuracy?\nbetter than 10 percent"}

    S2 -->|Yes| S3{"I2C interface\nacceptable?"}
    S2 -->|No| S4{"High-speed response\nrequired?"}

    S3 -->|Yes| BH["Use BH1750\n16-bit digital lux"]
    S3 -->|No| S5["Use photodiode + op-amp TIA"]

    S4 -->|Yes| PT["Use phototransistor TEMT6000"]
    S4 -->|No| S6["LDR is sufficient"]

    LDRP --> OUT["Suitable for KTU labs\nand academic demos"]
    BH --> OUT2["Suitable for production\nIoT deployment"]
    PT --> OUT2
    S5 --> OUT3["Suitable for research-grade\ninstruments"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define an ambient light sensor and list two common types used in IoT applications.**

**Model Answer:**

An **ambient light sensor (ALS)** is a photoelectronic transducer that converts the intensity of surrounding visible light into a corresponding electrical signal — typically an analog voltage or a digital lux count — for use in IoT perception systems.

**Two common types:**
1. **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** — photoconductive type; resistance drops as light rises; output read via voltage divider.
2. **Digital IC ALS (e.g., BH1750 / TSL2561)** — photodiode + integrated ADC + I²C interface; outputs calibrated lux values directly.

*Secondary valid answers: photodiode, phototransistor, CMOS image sensor (single-pixel mode), thermopile-based lux meter.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the voltage divider formula used to read an LDR via an analog pin and explain why a fixed resistor is required.**

**Model Answer:**

The voltage at the analog pin is given by:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

A **fixed resistor** is essential because an LDR is a *purely resistive* (two-terminal) component — it cannot, by itself, generate any measurable voltage. Connecting it in series with $R_{fixed}$ to form a divider creates a **voltage tap** whose potential varies predictably with changes in $R_{LDR}$, thereby transforming the resistance change into a quantifiable voltage signal readable by the microcontroller's ADC. Without $R_{fixed}$, the LDR would float electrically and no ADC reading would be possible.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat circuit diagram, explain how an **LDR-based ambient light sensing circuit** is interfaced with an Arduino Uno. Derive the expression for the analog output voltage and describe the role of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An Arduino Uno (10-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 5$ V) reads an LDR voltage divider with $R_{fixed} = 4.7$ kΩ. The ADC returns $D = 720$. Given the LDR model $R_{LDR} = 40000 \cdot L^{-0.85}$, calculate: (i) the analog voltage $V_{out}$, (ii) the LDR resistance, and (iii) the ambient illuminance in lux.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **LDR**, **photodiode**, and **digital ambient light sensor IC (BH1750)** under the headings: working principle, output type, linearity, interface complexity, and IoT suitability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **smart streetlight controller** using an ambient light sensor, ESP32 microcontroller, and a relay module. Explain the data flow from perception to actuation and write the pseudo-code for the threshold logic.

---

### **SOLUTION — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

**Circuit Description:**

The circuit consists of three main elements:

1. **LDR** — connected between **+5V (Arduino VCC)** and **analog input pin A0**.
2. **Fixed resistor** ($R_{fixed}$ = 4.7 kΩ or 10 kΩ typically) — connected between **A0** and **GND**.
3. **Arduino Uno** — reads the voltage at A0 using its internal 10-bit ADC.

**Working Principle:**

When light falls on the LDR, its resistance $R_{LDR}$ decreases (CdS photoconductivity effect). This shifts the voltage divider ratio in favor of the LDR branch, causing the voltage at A0 to **rise**. Conversely, in darkness, $R_{LDR}$ rises, pulling A0 toward 0 V.

**Derivation:**

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the divider loop:

$$V_{ref} = V_{R_{LDR}} + V_{R_{fixed}}$$

$$V_{R_{LDR}} = I \cdot R_{LDR}, \quad V_{R_{fixed}} = I \cdot R_{fixed}$$

The voltage at A0 equals the voltage drop across $R_{fixed}$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

> *(Note: depending on whether LDR is on top or bottom of the divider, the formula varies. The above assumes LDR is connected to GND and $R_{fixed}$ to VCC. KTU exam setters typically place LDR at top — student should always redraw the circuit before writing the formula.)*

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Given:** $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $R_{fixed} = 4.7$ kΩ, $D = 720$, model $R_{LDR} = 40000 \cdot L^{-0.85}$.

**(i) Compute $V_{out}$:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{D \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{720 \times 5}{1023} = \frac{3600}{1023}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 3.519 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Compute $R_{LDR}$:**

Assuming LDR is at the top (between VCC and A0):

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$$

$$3.519 = 5 \cdot \frac{4700}{R_{LDR} + 4700}$$

$$R_{LDR} + 4700 = \frac{5 \times 4700}{3.519} = \frac{23500}{3.519} \approx 6678.6$$

$$R_{LDR} \approx 6678.6 - 4700 \approx 1978.6 \text{ Ω}$$

$$\boxed{R_{LDR} \approx 1.98 \text{ kΩ}}$$

**(iii) Compute $L$:**

$$R_{LDR} = 40000 \cdot L^{-0.85}$$

$$L^{-0.85} = \frac{1978.6}{40000} = 0.04947$$

$$L^{0.85} = \frac{1}{0.04947} \approx 20.215$$

$$L = (20.215)^{1/0.85} = (20.215)^{1.1765}$$

$$\ln(20.215) \approx 3.0062$$

$$1.1765 \times 3.0062 \approx 3.5373$$

$$L = e^{3.5373} \approx 34.4 \text{ lux}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 34.4 \text{ lux}}$$

This corresponds to **dim indoor / late twilight** conditions.

---

### **SOLUTION — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Model Answer

| Parameter | LDR | Photodiode (BPW34) | BH1750 (Digital IC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Photoconductive — resistance drops with light | Photovoltaic/photoconductive — generates photocurrent | Integrated photodiode + 16-bit ADC + I²C |
| **Output Type** | Analog resistance (needs divider) | Analog current (needs op-amp) | Digital lux value via I²C |
| **Linearity** | Logarithmic, non-linear | Linear with proper biasing | Linear (built-in compensation) |
| **Interface Complexity** | Very simple (1 resistor) | Moderate (transimpedance amplifier) | Moderate (I²C driver code needed) |
| **IoT Suitability** | Educational / hobbyist | Research / precision analog | **Production-grade IoT** (best choice) |

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Smart Streetlight Controller Design:**

**Hardware Components:**
- **ESP32** microcontroller (Wi-Fi-enabled, dual-core, 12-bit ADC).
- **BH1750 / LDR module** connected to ESP32 via I²C (SDA → GPIO21, SCL → GPIO22) or analog pin A0.
- **5V SPDT Relay module** with optocoupler isolation.
- **12V LED streetlamp** powered via relay's normally-open (NO) contact.
- **3.3V regulated supply** for ESP32 (from USB or buck converter).

**Data Flow:**

```
Photons → LDR/BH1750 → ESP32 ADC/I2C → Lux Calculation →
Threshold Check → Relay GPIO Trigger → Lamp ON/OFF →
(Optional) MQTT Publish to cloud for remote monitoring
```

**Pseudo-code:**

```
initialize ESP32 Wi-Fi and I2C bus
configure GPIO 5 as OUTPUT for relay control
configure BH1750 in continuous high-resolution mode
THRESHOLD_ON  = 50     // lux below this → turn lamp ON
THRESHOLD_OFF = 200    // lux above this → turn lamp OFF

loop forever:
    lux_value = bh1750.readLightLevel()
    print "Ambient: ", lux_value, " lux"
    
    if lux_value < THRESHOLD_ON:
        digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, HIGH)    // energize relay coil
        lamp_state = ON
    else if lux_value > THRESHOLD_OFF:
        digitalWrite(RELAY_PIN, LOW)     // de-energize relay
        lamp_state = OFF
    
    // Hysteresis prevents relay chattering near threshold
    publish to MQTT broker: {"lux": lux_value, "lamp": lamp_state}
    
    delay 2000 milliseconds
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Inverting the divider formula** — many students write $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot R_{fixed} / (R_{LDR} + R_{fixed})$ even when the LDR is on top. **Always redraw the circuit and identify which resistor is connected to GND.**
> 2. **Forgetting the ADC offset** — using $2^n$ instead of $(2^n - 1)$ in the denominator. Full-scale count is $2^n - 1$, not $2^n$.
> 3. **Unit confusion in lux exponent** — the constant $A$ in $R = A \cdot L^\alpha$ carries units; if mixed, the result will be off by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **No hysteresis** in threshold logic — examiners specifically look for **separate ON and OFF thresholds** to demonstrate hysteresis understanding. Single-threshold designs will lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing lux with lumens** — lux is illuminance (lm/m²), lumens are total flux. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ Ambient light sensor = converts **light intensity → electrical signal**.
- ✅ Standard unit of illuminance = **lux (lx)** = **lumens / m²**.
- ✅ LDR working principle = **photoconductivity** in CdS semiconductor.
- ✅ LDR resistance **decreases** as light **increases** (inverse relationship).
- ✅ Voltage divider formula: $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}}$ (LDR on top branch).
- ✅ ADC conversion: $D = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$.
- ✅ Arduino Uno ADC = **10-bit (1024 levels)**, ESP32 = **12-bit (4096 levels)**.
- ✅ LDR empirical model: $R_{LDR} = A \cdot L^{\alpha}$, with $\alpha \approx -0.8$ for typical CdS.
- ✅ Inverse-square law: $E_v = I / d^2$ — illuminance falls as **square of distance**.
- ✅ Photon energy: $E = h\nu = hc / \lambda$ — higher frequency = higher energy photon.
- ✅ Standard lux reference values — daylight $\approx 10^4$ lx, office $\approx 500$ lx, moonlight $\approx 0.1$ lx.
- ✅ Use **hysteresis** (separate ON/OFF thresholds) in actuator switching logic.
- ✅ BH1750 / TSL2561 = preferred **production IoT** ambient light sensors (digital I²C).
- ✅ Always include **opto-isolated relay** when switching AC loads like streetlamps.
- ✅ KTU Module 4 context: this sensor belongs to the **Perception Layer** of the IoT stack.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating an Actuator for Controlling Illumination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST755 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Creating an Actuator for Controlling Illumination # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of the **Internet of Things (IoT)**, an **Actuator** is a physical output device that converts an electrical signal from a microcontroller or processing unit into a real-world physical action such as motion, heat, sound, or in this specific case, **Illumination (Light)**. The process of *Creating an Actuator for Controlling Illumination* refers to the engineering procedure of designing, wiring, programming, and integrating a light-emitting hardware component (typically an **LED**, **Light Bulb**, or **Smart Lamp**) with an IoT-enabled controller (Arduino UNO, ESP8266, NodeMCU, or Raspberry Pi) so that its ON/OFF state, intensity, color, or timing can be regulated remotely via the internet, a local network, or a sensor-driven feedback loop. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner's Wording):** > An actuator is the "effector" arm of an IoT system. While *sensors* are the **five senses** of an IoT node, *actuators* are its **hands and voice** — they physically alter the environment. Illumination control is the most classical, foundational actuator application in any introductory IoT syllabus. ## 1.2 The IoT Triad: Sensor → Network → Actuator To contextualize illumination control inside the IoT framework, it is essential to understand the data flow: 1. **Perception Layer (Sensor):** A light-dependent resistor (LDR) senses ambient brightness. 2. **Network Layer:** A Wi-Fi module (ESP8266) transmits data to the cloud. 3. **Application Layer (Actuator):** Based on threshold logic, the microcontroller drives an LED ON or adjusts its brightness automatically. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Dimmer Switch" Metaphor Imagine a traditional table lamp connected to a wall. To turn it on, you physically press a switch. Now, imagine placing a **tiny, intelligent robot finger** between the switch and the lamp. This robot finger: - Receives wireless instructions from your phone ("Turn ON to 60 % brightness"). - Touches the switch for a fraction of a second very rapidly to dim the light (this is **PWM — Pulse Width Modulation**). - Holds the switch down for full brightness. That "robot finger" is the **microcontroller + transistor + LED circuit**. Building this entire assembly — from scratch — is what *Creating an Actuator for Controlling Illumination* means. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this important for KTU exams?** > The question is rarely just "write a code." Examiners test whether you understand the **complete hardware-software-data pipeline**: GPIO pin selection → current-limiting resistor calculation → transistor driver stage (for high-power loads) → PWM duty cycle logic → cloud/MQTT/Blynk integration. Marks are distributed across all these layers. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Memorize) | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Standard LED Forward Voltage | $V_f$ | **2.0 V** (Red), **3.2 V** (Blue/White) | Volts (V) | | Standard LED Forward Current | $I_f$ | **20 mA** | Milliamperes (mA) | | Arduino UNO GPIO Output Voltage | $V_{OH}$ | **5 V** | Volts (V) | | Arduino UNO GPIO Source Current | $I_{OH}$ | **40 mA max / 20 mA recommended** | mA | | ESP8266 GPIO Logic Level | $V_{IO}$ | **3.3 V** | Volts (V) | | PWM Frequency (Arduino default) | $f_{PWM}$ | **~490 Hz** (pin 3,9,11) / **980 Hz** (pin 5,6) | Hertz (Hz) | | Human Eye Perceived Brightness Resolution | — | **8-bit (0–255)** standard | levels | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule for KTU:** The single most common mistake students make is connecting an LED **directly** to a 5 V pin without a resistor. This burns the LED within seconds. Always include a current-limiting resistor. The derivation of this resistor is provided in Section 3. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown: How an Illumination Actuator Works The construction of an illumination actuator follows a strict five-stage logical pipeline: ### Stage 1 — Selecting the Light Source - **Small-load application (< 100 mA):** Standard 5 mm or 3 mm LED (Red, Green, Blue, White, RGB). - **Medium-load application (100 mA – 1 A):** High-power 1 W / 3 W LED module with heat sink. - **High-load application (AC mains bulbs):** Incandescent, CFL, or LED bulb driven through a **relay module** or **TRIAC / SSR (Solid State Relay)**. ### Stage 2 — Microcontroller GPIO Selection The **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pin is the digital "tap" through which the controller outputs the control signal. For Arduino UNO: PWM-capable pins are **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** (marked with the `~` symbol on the board). These pins support the `analogWrite()` function. ### Stage 3 — Current-Limiting Resistor (For LED Only) A resistor is inserted in series with the LED to drop the excess voltage and protect the diode from thermal runaway. Without it, the LED draws infinite current and burns out. ### Stage 4 — Driver Stage (For High-Power Loads) Microcontroller GPIO pins cannot source more than **40 mA**. To switch a 12 V LED strip or a 230 V bulb, an intermediate driver is mandatory: - **BJT (e.g., 2N2222):** For low-side DC switching. - **MOSFET (e.g., IRF540N):** For fast PWM-switching high-current DC loads. - **Relay Module (5 V coil):** For AC mains switching (optically isolated for safety). - **SSR (Solid State Relay):** For silent, long-life AC switching. ### Stage 5 — Software Control Logic The microcontroller executes a program that: - Sets the GPIO as OUTPUT via `pinMode()`. - Writes HIGH/LOW via `digitalWrite()` for ON/OFF control. - Writes a value 0–255 via `analogWrite()` for brightness control via PWM. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind PWM:** A digital pin can only output 0 V (LOW) or 5 V (HIGH) — it cannot natively output "2.5 V". PWM fakes an analog voltage by switching the pin HIGH and LOW so rapidly that the **average voltage** seen by the LED appears as 2.5 V, dimming it. This is the **Illumination Control Actuator** in its purest form. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet The following formulas are the **only mathematical tools** required to solve any illumination-actuator numerical in the KTU ESE. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ohm's Law (Resistor Value) | $R = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_f}$ | $V_{supply}$ = source voltage, $V_f$ = LED forward voltage, $I_f$ = LED forward current | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 2 | Power Dissipated by Resistor | $P_R = I_f^2 \cdot R$ | $P_R$ = power lost as heat in resistor | Watts (W) | | 3 | Power Consumed by LED | $P_{LED} = V_f \cdot I_f$ | Optical + heat power at the diode | Watts (W) | | 4 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D\,(\%) = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$ | $T_{ON}$ = HIGH time, $T_{OFF}$ = LOW time | Percentage (%) | | 5 | Average PWM Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | Equivalent DC voltage perceived by load | Volts (V) | | 6 | analogWrite() Duty Mapping | $D\,(\%) = \dfrac{\text{value}}{255} \times 100$ | value is an integer between 0 and 255 | Percentage (%) | | 7 | BJT Base Resistor (2N2222) | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{IO} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$ | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$, $I_B = I_C / h_{FE}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 8 | LED Current (Series Chain) | $I_{chain} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - n \cdot V_f}{R}$ | $n$ = number of LEDs in series | Amperes (A) | | 9 | Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | Read from relay datasheet | mA | | 10 | Frequency-to-Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{PWM}}$ | $f_{PWM} \approx 490\,\text{Hz}$ default Arduino | Seconds (s) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** Numerical problems in KTU ESE typically ask one of three questions: > (i) Calculate the current-limiting resistor for a given LED. > (ii) Find the analogWrite() value required for a desired brightness percentage. > (iii) Calculate the power dissipated. Memorize equations 1, 4, and 6 — these cover ~90 % of the numerical questions. ## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World IoT The illumination actuator you build in this lab module is the **direct ancestor** of: - **Philips Hue** smart bulbs (uses ZigBee + PWM triac dimming). - **LIFX** Wi-Fi smart LEDs (uses ESP32 + MOSFET + PWM for RGB control). - **Smart street-lighting systems** in Smart City projects (uses LDR sensor + relay + GSM/WiFi). - **Greenhouse automated grow lights** in precision agriculture (uses soil moisture + temperature sensors + LED driver). Thus, mastering this single module unlocks the design pattern for virtually all commercial smart-lighting products. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step mathematical derivations for the three most frequently asked numerical problems on "Creating an Actuator for Controlling Illumination" in the KTU ESE. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current-Limiting Resistor for a Single LED ### Problem Statement An LED with a forward voltage $V_f = 2.0\,\text{V}$ and a maximum forward current $I_f = 20\,\text{mA}$ is connected to an Arduino UNO digital pin outputting 5 V. Calculate the value of the series current-limiting resistor. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify Known Quantities** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{supply} &= 5\,\text{V} \quad \text{(Arduino HIGH output)} \\ V_f &= 2.0\,\text{V} \quad \text{(Red LED forward voltage from datasheet)} \\ I_f &= 20\,\text{mA} = 0.020\,\text{A} \quad \text{(desired operating current)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** Around the series loop (Resistor + LED), the sum of voltage drops must equal the supply voltage: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{supply} &= V_R + V_f \\ V_R &= V_{supply} - V_f \\ V_R &= 5\,\text{V} - 2.0\,\text{V} \\ V_R &= 3.0\,\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ The resistor must "absorb" the **3.0 V** difference to protect the LED. **Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= \dfrac{V_R}{I_f} \\ R &= \dfrac{3.0\,\text{V}}{0.020\,\text{A}} \\ R &= 150\,\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Choose the Next Standard E12 Resistor Value** E12 standard series values: 10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, 39, 47, 56, 68, 82, 100, 120, **150**, 180, 220… Since 150 Ω is itself a standard E12 value, we can directly use **150 Ω**. However, to slightly reduce current and extend LED life, a **220 Ω** resistor is the most common engineering choice. **Step 5 — Recompute Actual Current with 220 Ω** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{actual} &= \dfrac{V_R}{R} = \dfrac{3.0\,\text{V}}{220\,\Omega} \\ I_{actual} &\approx 0.01364\,\text{A} = 13.64\,\text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ This is well within the safe operating range (typically ≤ 20 mA), giving a long LED lifespan. **Step 6 — Power Dissipation Check** $$ \begin{aligned} P_R &= I_{actual}^2 \cdot R \\ P_R &= (0.01364)^2 \cdot 220 \\ P_R &= 0.0409\,\text{W} \approx 41\,\text{mW} \end{aligned} $$ A standard **¼ W (0.25 W)** resistor is more than adequate. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — analogWrite() Value for a Desired Brightness ### Problem Statement A user wants the LED to appear at **60 %** perceived brightness. What integer value should be passed to `analogWrite(pin, value)`? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Recall the Duty Cycle Mapping** Arduino's `analogWrite()` accepts an 8-bit integer from 0 to 255. The percentage duty cycle is: $$ D\,(\%) = \dfrac{\text{value}}{255} \times 100 $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to Solve for "value"** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{value} &= \dfrac{D\,(\%)}{100} \times 255 \\ \text{value} &= \dfrac{60}{100} \times 255 \\ \text{value} &= 0.60 \times 255 \\ \text{value} &= 153 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Round to Nearest Integer** Since `analogWrite()` accepts only integers, the value **153** is passed. The actual duty cycle becomes: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{actual} &= \dfrac{153}{255} \times 100 \\ D_{actual} &\approx 60.0\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Verify the Equivalent Analog Voltage** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{avg} &= D_{actual} \times V_{supply} \\ V_{avg} &= 0.60 \times 5\,\text{V} \\ V_{avg} &= 3.0\,\text{V} \end{aligned} $$ The LED effectively "sees" 3.0 V average, which is why it dims to 60 % brightness. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Budget for an RGB LED Strip Actuator ### Problem Statement An RGB LED strip contains 60 LEDs per meter, each color channel drawing 20 mA. A 2-meter strip is driven by an ESP8266 via a MOSFET at 12 V. Calculate total current, total power, and required MOSFET rating. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Total LED Count** $$ \begin{aligned} N_{total} &= 60\,\frac{\text{LEDs}}{\text{m}} \times 2\,\text{m} \\ N_{total} &= 120\,\text{LEDs per channel} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Current Per Color Channel** Assuming all 120 LEDs of one color are ON simultaneously: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{channel} &= N_{total} \times I_{f} \\ I_{channel} &= 120 \times 20\,\text{mA} \\ I_{channel} &= 2400\,\text{mA} = 2.4\,\text{A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total Current (All Three Colors at Full White)** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{total} &= 3 \times I_{channel} \\ I_{total} &= 3 \times 2.4\,\text{A} \\ I_{total} &= 7.2\,\text{A} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Total Power Dissipated** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{total} &= V_{supply} \times I_{total} \\ P_{total} &= 12\,\text{V} \times 7.2\,\text{A} \\ P_{total} &= 86.4\,\text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Required MOSFET Rating** Following the 80 % derating rule for reliability: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{MOSFET,min} &= \dfrac{I_{total}}{0.80} \\ I_{MOSFET,min} &= \dfrac{7.2\,\text{A}}{0.80} \\ I_{MOSFET,min} &= 9.0\,\text{A} \end{aligned} $$ Hence, a **12 A or higher rated logic-level MOSFET** (e.g., IRLZ44N or IRL540N) is recommended. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight for Examiners:** This shows why a bare microcontroller GPIO **cannot** drive an LED strip directly — 7.2 A far exceeds the 40 mA pin limit. This justifies the use of a MOSFET driver stage. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Actuator Configuration to Use? | Load Type | Current Draw | Recommended Driver | PWM Capable? | Cost | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Single 5 mm LED | ~ 20 mA | Direct GPIO + Resistor | Yes | ₹ 5 | Status indicator | | High-Power 1 W LED | ~ 350 mA | MOSFET (IRF540N) | Yes | ₹ 25 | Torch / Lamp | | 12 V LED Strip (1 m) | ~ 400 mA | MOSFET + 12 V supply | Yes | ₹ 80 | Cove lighting | | 12 V RGB Strip (2 m) | ~ 7.2 A | 3× MOSFET (RGB) | Yes (per channel) | ₹ 250 | Ambient lighting | | 230 V AC Bulb | up to 1 A | Relay module (5 V coil) | NO (digital only) | ₹ 60 | Room lighting | | 230 V AC Dimmer | varies | TRIAC / SSR + zero-cross | Yes | ₹ 350 | Smart dimmer | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics The following Mermaid diagrams render the **complete functional architecture** of an IoT-driven illumination actuator system, as would be expected in a KTU board answer script. ## 4.1 High-Level System Architecture (Sensor → Cloud → Actuator) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[LDR Sensor
Perceives Ambient Light] --> B[ESP8266 MCU
Analog Input A0] B --> C[WiFi Router
Gateway] C --> D[Cloud MQTT Broker
e.g., HiveMQ / AWS IoT] D --> E[Mobile App Dashboard
Blynk / Custom HTML] E -->|Publish Command
topic:cmnd/light/PWM| D D -->|Subscribe Message| F[ESP8266 Actuator Node
GPIO Pin D5] F --> G[MOSFET Driver
IRF540N] G --> H[LED Strip Load
12V Illumination] H -->|Light Output| I[Illuminated Environment] I -.->|Ambient Light Change| A classDef sensor fill:#FFF4E1,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef process fill:#E1F0FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000 classDef actuator fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 class A,I sensor class B,C,D,E,F process class G,H actuator ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Hardware Wiring Flow (Arduino UNO + LED + Resistor) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Arduino UNO
Digital Pin 9 PWM] -->|Wire Red 5V Signal| B[220 Ohm Resistor
R1 Current Limiter] B -->|Voltage Drop 3.0V| C[LED Anode
Positive Terminal] C --> D[LED Cathode
Negative Terminal] D -->|Return Path| E[Arduino GND Pin
Common Ground] F[External 12V DC Supply
For High-Power Loads] -->|V+ Rail| G[MOSFET Drain Pin] H[Arduino Pin 9] -->|Gate Drive Signal
via 100 Ohm| I[MOSFET Gate Pin] I --> J[MOSFET Source Pin] J -->|Return Path| K[External GND Rail] K --> E G --> L[LED Strip Anode] L --> M[LED Strip Cathode] M --> K classDef arduino fill:#00979D,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef component fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef rail fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#666,color:#000 class A,H arduino class B,C,D,G,I,J,L,M component class E,F,K rail ``` ## 4.3 Software Control Flow (Firmware Decision Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B[setup function
pinMode pin 9 OUTPUT
pinMode pin A0 INPUT] B --> C[Connect WiFi
SSID + Password] C --> D[Connect MQTT Broker
client.connect] D --> E[Subscribe Topic
iot/light/control] E --> F{loop function
Message Received?} F -->|No| G[Read LDR Sensor
analogRead A0] G --> H{lux Value
less than Threshold} H -->|Yes| I[analogWrite Pin 9
255 Full Brightness] H -->|No| J[analogWrite Pin 9
0 LED OFF] I --> F J --> F F -->|Yes| K[Parse Payload
e.g. ON, OFF, 60] K --> L{Command Type} L -->|ON| M[analogWrite 9, 255] L -->|OFF| N[analogWrite 9, 0] L -->|Number 0 to 100| O[Convert to 0 to 255
map function] O --> P[analogWrite 9, PWM Value] M --> F N --> F P --> F classDef terminal fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef io fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 class A terminal class B,C,D,E,G,I,J,K,M,N,O,P io class F,H,L decision ``` ## 4.4 Reference Arduino Sketch (Verbatim KTU Lab Code Pattern) ```cpp // ===================================================== // KTU PECST755 - Module 4 Lab Reference // IoT Illumination Actuator with PWM Brightness Control // Platform: Arduino UNO / ESP8266 (NodeMCU) // ===================================================== int ledPin = 9; // PWM-capable pin int ldrPin = A0; // LDR sensor analog input int threshold = 400; // Dark/Light threshold (0-1023) // ---------- Setup ---------- void setup() { pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); pinMode(ldrPin, INPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } // ---------- Main Loop ---------- void loop() { int luxValue = analogRead(ldrPin); Serial.print("Ambient Light Level: "); Serial.println(luxValue); // -------- Automatic Mode Logic -------- if (luxValue < threshold) { // Dark environment -> turn LED ON at 75% brightness int pwmValue = map(75, 0, 100, 0, 255); // 75% -> 191 analogWrite(ledPin, pwmValue); Serial.println("LED ON - 75% PWM"); } else { // Bright environment -> LED OFF analogWrite(ledPin, 0); Serial.println("LED OFF"); } delay(500); // Sample every 500 ms } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key function `map()`:** `map(value, fromLow, fromHigh, toLow, toHigh)` is the most tested function in KTU labs. It linearly scales one range to another — here it converts a percentage (0–100) to the PWM range (0–255). Memorize its signature. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Define an actuator in the context of IoT. Differentiate between an actuator and a sensor with one example each related to illumination control.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** An **actuator** is an output hardware component that converts electrical signals from a microcontroller into a physical action upon the environment. In contrast, a **sensor** is an input device that converts a physical parameter into an electrical signal readable by the controller. - *Example Sensor for Illumination:* **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** — senses ambient light intensity and outputs a varying resistance. - *Example Actuator for Illumination:* **LED or Smart Bulb** — receives an electrical signal and produces light as output. Thus, sensors *perceive* and actuators *act upon* the environment. *(3 Marks: 1 definition + 1 each example + 0.5 distinction + 0.5 conclusion)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: What is Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)? Why is it essential for controlling illumination brightness using a microcontroller?** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is a digital signaling technique in which a square wave of fixed frequency is generated with a variable ratio of ON-time to total period, called the **duty cycle**. The duty cycle $D$ determines the average voltage delivered to the load: $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$. It is essential because microcontroller GPIO pins can only output HIGH (5 V) or LOW (0 V) — they **cannot natively generate an intermediate analog voltage**. PWM fakes an analog level by rapidly switching, allowing smooth **brightness dimming** of LEDs from 0 % to 100 %. The Arduino function `analogWrite(pin, value)` with `value ∈ [0, 255]` controls this duty cycle. *(3 Marks: 1 PWM definition + 1 average voltage formula + 1 digital-to-analog reasoning)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question B (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: Design and explain an IoT-based illumination control system using Arduino UNO. Your answer must include:** **(a)** Circuit diagram with proper component selection for driving a 12 V LED strip using a MOSFET. Calculate the current-limiting resistor for the gate if $V_{GS} = 5\,\text{V}$ and required gate current is 10 mA. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Write the complete Arduino sketch to read an LDR sensor and automatically control LED brightness using PWM. Explain the role of the `map()` function. *(7 Marks)* --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Component Selection Table (1 Mark):** | Component | Specification | Reason | |---|---|---| | Arduino UNO | ATmega328P, 5 V logic | Standard KTU lab platform | | N-Channel MOSFET | IRF540N / IRLZ44N | Logic-level, 33 A rated | | LED Strip | 12 V, 60 LEDs/m | High-power load | | Gate Resistor $R_G$ | 470 Ω | Limits inrush current | | Pull-down Resistor $R_{PD}$ | 10 kΩ | Keeps MOSFET OFF during reset | | Power Supply | 12 V, 2 A SMPS | Adequate current budget | **Circuit Description (2 Marks):** Arduino digital pin D9 (PWM) connects via a 470 Ω resistor to the **Gate** of the MOSFET. The **Source** pin goes to common GND. The **Drain** pin connects to the LED strip's negative terminal, whose positive terminal goes to the +12 V supply. An LDR voltage divider (LDR + 10 kΩ) feeds analog pin A0. **Resistor Calculation (4 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{GS} &= 5\,\text{V} \quad \text{(Arduino output)} \\ V_{GateResistor} &= V_{GS} - V_{GateThreshold} \\ &\approx 5\,\text{V} - 0\,\text{V} \quad \text{(assuming Vth negligible when fully ON)} \\ V_{R_G} &= 5\,\text{V} \\ I_G &= 10\,\text{mA} = 0.010\,\text{A} \\ R_G &= \dfrac{V_{R_G}}{I_G} = \dfrac{5}{0.010} = 500\,\Omega \end{aligned} $$ Use nearest standard **470 Ω** resistor. *(Full Marks: 1 for given data, 1 for KVL application, 1 for Ohm's law, 1 for E-series selection)* --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Arduino Sketch (5 Marks):** ```cpp int ledPin = 9; // PWM output int ldrPin = A0; // LDR analog input int pwmOut = 0; void setup() { pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int sensorValue = analogRead(ldrPin); // Map LDR range 0-1023 to PWM range 0-255 // Reverse mapping: dark LDR = high PWM (LED ON bright) pwmOut = map(sensorValue, 0, 1023, 255, 0); // Constrain to safe PWM range pwmOut = constrain(pwmOut, 0, 255); analogWrite(ledPin, pwmOut); Serial.print("LDR: "); Serial.print(sensorValue); Serial.print(" PWM: "); Serial.println(pwmOut); delay(200); } ``` **Explanation of `map()` (2 Marks):** The `map(value, fromLow, fromHigh, toLow, toHigh)` function linearly re-maps a number from one range to another. Here, it converts the 10-bit ADC reading (0–1023) to an 8-bit PWM value (0–255). The reversed order (255, 0) ensures that **low light → high brightness** (dark = LED bright). Without `map()`, manual arithmetic would be error-prone and unreadable. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Q: Answer the following in detail:** **(a)** Explain the working principle of an LDR-based automatic streetlight system. Draw the circuit and describe the role of each component. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A 5 V supply drives a white LED ($V_f = 3.2\,\text{V}$, $I_f = 20\,\text{mA}$). Calculate the series resistor value and the power dissipated by it. Justify why a BJT or MOSFET is not required in this case but becomes mandatory for a 12 V LED strip. *(7 Marks)* --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Working Principle (3 Marks):** An **automatic streetlight** uses an LDR to detect ambient daylight. During daytime, the LDR resistance is low (few hundred ohms), causing the voltage divider output to exceed the threshold. The microcontroller reads this and turns the streetlight OFF. At night, the LDR resistance rises to megaohms, voltage at A0 drops below threshold, and the microcontroller drives a relay or MOSFET to switch ON the streetlight. A hysteresis band (e.g., turn-on at <300, turn-off at >700) prevents rapid toggling at dawn/dusk. **Circuit Description (2 Marks):** - **LDR + 10 kΩ** voltage divider feeds analog pin A0. - **Arduino Pin 9 (PWM)** drives the **Gate of IRF540N MOSFET**. - **MOSFET Drain** switches the **12 V streetlight LED** (or relay coil). - **Flyback diode (1N4007)** protects the MOSFET from inductive kickback if a relay is used. **Component Role Table (2 Marks):** | Component | Role | |---|---| | LDR | Light sensor (input) | | 10 kΩ resistor | Forms voltage divider with LDR | | Arduino UNO | Decision-making controller | | IRF540N | High-current driver switch | | 1N4007 Diode | Protects against back-EMF | | 12 V LED | The actuator (output illumination) | --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Resistor Calculation (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_R &= V_{supply} - V_f \\ V_R &= 5\,\text{V} - 3.2\,\text{V} = 1.8\,\text{V} \\ R &= \dfrac{V_R}{I_f} = \dfrac{1.8\,\text{V}}{0.020\,\text{A}} \\ R &= 90\,\Omega \end{aligned} $$ Nearest standard E12 value: **100 Ω** (to be safe, slightly under-drive the LED). **Step 2 — Power Dissipation (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_R &= I_f^2 \times R \\ P_R &= (0.020)^2 \times 100 \\ P_R &= 0.040\,\text{W} = 40\,\text{mW} \end{aligned} $$ A standard **¼ W resistor** is sufficient (derating factor of 6×). **Step 3 — Justification (2 Marks):** For a single 5 V / 20 mA LED, the **20 mA current is well within the Arduino GPIO's 40 mA limit**, and the 5 V supply matches the Arduino's logic level. Thus, a **direct GPIO connection with a current-limiting resistor suffices**. However, for a **12 V LED strip drawing ~400 mA**, the GPIO cannot source this current (40 mA limit), and the 12 V supply exceeds the Arduino's 5 V logic. Therefore, a **MOSFET or BJT driver stage** is mandatory — the MOSFET's Gate is driven by the 5 V Arduino signal, but its Drain-Source channel switches the entire 12 V / 400 mA load, isolating and amplifying the control signal. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the pull-down resistor on MOSFET Gate:** Without a 10 kΩ pull-down, the MOSFET may float into partial conduction during Arduino boot-up, causing the LED to flicker unpredictably. *(Lose 1 mark)* > 2. **Using $V_f = 2.0$ V for a white LED:** White and blue LEDs have $V_f \approx 3.0$–3.4 V. Using red LED voltage gives wrong resistor values. *(Lose 1 mark)* > 3. **Forgetting to call `pinMode()`:** A very common coding error — without setting the pin as OUTPUT, `digitalWrite()` has no effect. *(Lose 1 mark)* > 4. **Writing `analogWrite(pin, 300)`:** Values above 255 silently break PWM logic. Use `constrain()`. *(Lose 0.5 mark)* > 5. **Connecting 12 V directly to Arduino:** Instant board destruction. Always use a MOSFET or relay. *(Lose full marks for circuit safety)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Memorize Concepts for the KTU ESE (Module 4):** - ✅ An **actuator** is an **output device**; a **sensor** is an **input device**. Illumination actuators include LEDs, bulbs, and LED strips. - ✅ The **standard LED current-limiting resistor formula** is $R = (V_{supply} - V_f) / I_f$. For a 5 V red LED, this gives **150–220 Ω**. - ✅ **Arduino PWM pins** are 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 (marked `~`). - ✅ `analogWrite(pin, value)` accepts **0–255**, mapping to **0–100 % duty cycle**. - ✅ **PWM frequency** on most Arduino pins is **~490 Hz**, fast enough to avoid visible flicker. - ✅ The **average voltage** from PWM is $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$. - ✅ Use a **MOSFET (IRF540N / IRLZ44N)** for DC loads > 200 mA, and a **Relay / SSR** for AC mains. - ✅ Always include a **gate pull-down resistor (10 kΩ)** to prevent MOSFET floating during boot. - ✅ For AC mains, use an **optically isolated relay** for safety. - ✅ The `map(value, fromLow, fromHigh, toLow, toHigh)` function scales values across ranges — used extensively for sensor-to-actuator mapping. - ✅ The LDR-based **automatic streetlight** is the canonical KTU lab viva question — be ready to draw the voltage divider and explain dark/light threshold logic. - ✅ An RGB LED strip requires **3 MOSFETs** (one per color channel) for independent dimming. - ✅ Power calculations: $P = V \times I$; for safety, derate component ratings by **20–25 %**. - ✅ Never connect a high-current or high-voltage load directly to a microcontroller GPIO pin. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Creating an Actuator for Controlling Illumination**

topics.title: Publishing Information Using MQTT & HTTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST755 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Publishing Information Using MQTT & HTTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, publish-subscribe based messaging protocol standardized under **ISO/IEC 20922**, designed for constrained devices and low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks. It operates over TCP/IP (typically port **1883** for unencrypted, **8883** for TLS) using a central **Broker** that decouples Publishers from Subscribers.

**HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)** is a request-response, stateless application-layer protocol standardized under **RFC 9110**, operating over TCP (port **80** for unencrypted, **443** for HTTPS via TLS). In IoT, HTTP is used to publish sensor data to RESTful Web Services (Cloud APIs) using methods like `POST` and `PUT`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In IoT, "publishing information" means making sensor-generated data available to consumers (other devices, cloud servers, dashboards). MQTT and HTTP are the two dominant Application Layer protocols used to achieve this in the KTU-recommended architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**MQTT Analogy — The Radio Station:**
Imagine a **FM Radio Station (Broker)**. A musician (Publisher/IoT Sensor) sings into the station's microphone. The station broadcasts the song over a specific frequency, say **93.5 MHz (Topic)**. Any car radio tuned to 93.5 MHz (Subscriber) automatically hears the song. The musician does not need to know who is listening, and the listener does not need to know the musician. The radio station mediates them.

**HTTP Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter:**
Imagine a customer (Client/IoT Device) sitting at a table. To get food (Data), the customer must call the **Waiter (HTTP Server)**, place a specific order `POST /api/temperature {value: 25}`, and wait for the waiter to return with the food. The customer must know the kitchen's menu (API endpoints), and the kitchen only serves the customer who asked. It is a **direct, one-to-one, request-driven** interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Difference:** MQTT is **event-driven and asynchronous** (push-based, fire-and-forget). HTTP is **request-driven and synchronous** (pull-based, must ask to receive).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | MQTT | HTTP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default Port** | **1883** / **8883** (TLS) | **80** / **443** (HTTPS) |
| **Standard** | **ISO/IEC 20922** | **RFC 9110** |
| **Architecture** | **Publish–Subscribe** | **Request–Response** |
| **Header Size** | **2 bytes** (fixed) | Variable, often **> 200 bytes** |
| **Transport** | TCP | TCP |
| **Year Introduced** | **1999** (IBM) | **1991** (Tim Berners-Lee) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 MQTT Operational Theory — The Publish/Subscribe Mechanism

MQTT uses a **Broker-centric topology** with three core entities: **Publisher**, **Subscriber**, and **Broker**.

**Logical Flow Steps:**

1. **Connection Establishment:** The IoT client opens a TCP connection to the broker on port 1883 and sends a `CONNECT` packet containing `ClientID`, `KeepAlive` timer, `Clean Session` flag, and authentication credentials.
2. **Subscription:** A subscriber sends a `SUBSCRIBE` packet listing one or more **Topics** (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`) with a desired **QoS level**.
3. **Publishing:** A sensor (Publisher) sends a `PUBLISH` packet to the broker containing the **Topic Name**, **Payload** (the actual sensor data), and **QoS level**.
4. **Broker Routing:** The broker matches the topic against all active subscriptions using **Topic Wildcards** (`+` for single level, `#` for multi-level) and forwards the message to all matching subscribers.
5. **Acknowledgment:** Depending on QoS, `PUBACK`, `PUBREC`, `PUBREL`, and `PUBCOMP` packets ensure reliable delivery.
6. **Keep-Alive:** The client must send a `PINGREQ` packet within 1.5 × `KeepAlive` seconds to maintain the connection; otherwise, the broker assumes disconnection.

**Quality of Service (QoS) Levels — The 'Why' Behind Reliability:**

- **QoS 0 — At Most Once:** Fire-and-forget. No acknowledgment. Lowest overhead. Used for non-critical telemetry.
- **QoS 1 — At Least Once:** Uses `PUBACK` handshake. Message may be delivered multiple times. Used for critical data where duplicates are tolerable.
- **QoS 2 — Exactly Once:** Four-step handshake (`PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP`). Highest reliability. Used for billing, medical, or mission-critical data.

### 2.2 HTTP Operational Theory — The RESTful Publish Model

In IoT, HTTP is used to publish data to **REST APIs** (Representational State Transfer). Each IoT resource (e.g., a sensor) is identified by a **URL** and manipulated using standard methods.

**Logical Flow Steps:**

1. **TCP Handshake + TLS (if HTTPS):** The client establishes a secure channel with the cloud server.
2. **HTTP Request Formation:** The device constructs an HTTP request with a method, URL, headers (Content-Type, Authorization), and body (JSON payload).
3. **Server Processing:** The REST server (e.g., a Flask/Node.js app on AWS) parses the request, authenticates the device (OAuth/API Key), validates the JSON schema, and persists data to a database.
4. **HTTP Response:** The server returns a **Status Code** (e.g., `201 Created` for successful POST, `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`).
5. **Statelessness:** Each request is independent; the server does not retain client state between requests.

**Core HTTP Methods for IoT Publishing:**

- **`POST /api/sensors/data`** — Create a new sensor reading.
- **`PUT /api/sensors/{id}`** — Update/replace an existing sensor record.
- **`GET /api/sensors/{id}`** — Retrieve data (read-only, not a publish action).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | MQTT | HTTP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture** | Pub/Sub (Broker-mediated) | Request/Response (Client-Server) |
| **Message Size Overhead** | **2 bytes** fixed header | **~200–800 bytes** typical header |
| **Typical Payload Format** | Binary or UTF-8 string | **JSON / XML / CBOR** |
| **QoS / Reliability** | 3 levels (0, 1, 2) | TCP guarantees only; retries at app layer |
| **Connection Lifetime** | Long-lived (Keep-Alive) | Short-lived (per request) or Keep-Alive |
| **Power Consumption** | **Low** (ideal for battery devices) | **High** (frequent handshakes) |
| **Topic Structure** | Hierarchical with `/` separator | URL path `/api/zone/sensor` |
| **Security** | TLS + Username/Password + X.509 | HTTPS + OAuth 2.0 + JWT + API Keys |
| **Best Use Case** | Real-time telemetry, constrained devices | Cloud APIs, web dashboards, firmware OTA |
| **Key Component** | **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) | **Web Server** (e.g., Nginx, AWS API Gateway) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** MQTT dominates **constrained IoT** (e.g., soil moisture sensors on solar power), while HTTP dominates **cloud-edge integration** (e.g., ESP32 posting to AWS IoT Core via HTTPS REST). Hybrid systems like **AWS IoT Core** natively support both.

### 2.4 Mathematical Formulation — MQTT Keep-Alive Constraint

The broker enforces a connection timeout to detect dead clients. The relationship is governed by:

$$
T_{\text{timeout}} = 1.5 \times K_{\text{alive}}
$$

where $K_{\text{alive}}$ is the **Keep-Alive interval** (in seconds) negotiated during the `CONNECT` handshake. The client must transmit at least one control packet (data or `PINGREQ`) within $K_{\text{alive}}$ seconds. The broker, upon detecting $1.5 \times K_{\text{alive}}$ seconds of silence, will close the connection and dispatch a **Last Will and Testament (LWT)** message if configured.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — MQTT vs HTTP Publishing Decision Tree

The selection of MQTT or HTTP for publishing IoT data follows a structured engineering decision matrix:

| Criterion | Choose MQTT | Choose HTTP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Device Power Budget** | < 1000 mAh battery | Mains-powered or rechargeable |
| **Network Reliability** | Unstable, intermittent (LPWAN, cellular) | Stable broadband / Wi-Fi |
| **Data Frequency** | High-frequency, periodic telemetry | Event-driven, sporadic uploads |
| **Number of Consumers** | Many (one-to-many fan-out) | One (client-to-server) |
| **Real-Time Latency** | < 1 second required | Seconds-to-minutes acceptable |
| **Existing Infrastructure** | Broker available (HiveMQ, EMQX) | REST API already deployed |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — HTTP Request Packet Construction for IoT Publishing

When an IoT device (e.g., a NodeMCU with DHT11 sensor) publishes a temperature reading of **25.4 °C** and humidity of **60%** to a cloud REST API, the request packet is constructed as follows:

**Step 1: Select HTTP Method and Endpoint.**
The device is creating a *new* sensor record, so it uses `POST`. The cloud service exposes the endpoint `/api/v1/sensors/data`.

**Step 2: Construct Request Line.**
The first line of an HTTP request is the **Request Line**, consisting of Method, Request-URI, and HTTP-Version, separated by spaces and terminated by CRLF (`\r\n`).

$$
\text{Request-Line} = \text{Method} \; \text{SP} \; \text{Request-URI} \; \text{SP} \; \text{HTTP-Version} \; \text{CRLF}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{Request-Line} = \text{POST} \; / \text{api/v1/sensors/data} \; \text{HTTP/1.1} \r\n
$$

**Step 3: Construct Header Fields.**
Each header is a key-value pair terminated by CRLF. The standard headers for IoT publishing are:
- `Host`: identifies the virtual host.
- `Content-Type`: specifies the payload media type (JSON for IoT).
- `Content-Length`: size of the body in bytes (essential for the server to parse).
- `Authorization`: bearer token for authentication.

$$
\text{Headers} = \text{Host: iot.example.com}\r\n \text{Content-Type: application/json}\r\n \text{Content-Length: 47}\r\n \text{Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGc...}\r\n
$$

**Step 4: Construct the JSON Payload Body.**
The sensor data is serialized into JSON. The logical structure is:

$$
\text{Body} = \{\text{"deviceId": "}\vert\text{D001}\vert\text{"}, \text{"ts": } \vert t \vert\text{, "temp": } \vert T \vert\text{, "hum": } \vert H \vert\text{\}}
$$

Substituting the actual measurements (assume timestamp $t = 1718901234$, temperature $T = 25.4$, humidity $H = 60$):

$$
\text{Body} = \{"deviceId":"D001","ts":1718901234,"temp":25.4,"hum":60\}
$$

**Step 5: Verify Content-Length.**
The byte count of the JSON string is exactly **47 bytes**, matching the `Content-Length` header. This is critical; any mismatch causes a `400 Bad Request`.

**Step 6: Final Assembled HTTP Request Packet.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Full Request} = & \; \text{POST /api/v1/sensors/data HTTP/1.1}\r\n \\
& \; \text{Host: iot.example.com}\r\n \\
& \; \text{Content-Type: application/json}\r\n \\
& \; \text{Content-Length: 47}\r\n \\
& \; \text{Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGc...}\r\n \\
& \; \r\n \\
& \; \{"deviceId":"D001","ts":1718901234,"temp":25.4,"hum":60\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Server Response (Asynchronous Acknowledgment).**
The cloud server, upon successful ingestion, replies with a `201 Created` status:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Response} = & \; \text{HTTP/1.1 201 Created}\r\n \\
& \; \text{Content-Type: application/json}\r\n \\
& \; \text{Content-Length: 32}\r\n \\
& \; \r\n \\
& \; \{"status":"success","id":"rec\_9f2a"\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — MQTT PUBLISH Packet Structure (2-Byte Fixed Header)

The MQTT `PUBLISH` packet demonstrates why MQTT is **bandwidth-efficient** for IoT. The fixed header is only **2 bytes** (for QoS 0).

**Bit-Level Breakdown of the Fixed Header:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 1} &= \text{Control Packet Type (4 bits)} \mid \text{Flags (4 bits)} \\
\text{Byte 2} &= \text{Remaining Length (variable, 1–4 bytes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Determine Control Packet Type.**
Per the MQTT specification, `PUBLISH` is packet type **3** (`0011` in binary). This occupies the **upper 4 bits** of Byte 1.

**Step 2: Set the Flags (Lower 4 bits of Byte 1).**
The flags are `DUP | QoS | RETAIN`, occupying bits 1, 2-3, and 4 of the lower nibble respectively:
- `DUP` (Duplicate) = **0** (first delivery).
- `QoS` = **01** (QoS Level 1, at-least-once).
- `RETAIN` = **0** (do not store on broker).

Concatenating: `0011 0010` in binary = `0x32` in hex = **50** in decimal.

**Step 3: Calculate Remaining Length.**
The "Remaining Length" is the byte count of the *Variable Header + Payload* that follows the fixed header. It uses a **Variable-Length Encoding** where each byte uses 7 bits for data and the MSB as a continuation flag.

For an example payload `{"t":25.4}` of 9 bytes, plus a variable header containing a 14-byte Topic Name (e.g., `home/room1/temp` is 14 chars → 2-byte length prefix + 14 bytes = 16 bytes) and a 2-byte Packet Identifier:

$$
L_{\text{remaining}} = L_{\text{topic-len}} + L_{\text{topic}} + L_{\text{pkt-id}} + L_{\text{payload}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{remaining}} = 2 + 14 + 2 + 9 = 27 \text{ bytes}
$$

Since 27 < 128, it fits in a single byte: `0001 1011` = `0x1B`.

**Step 4: Final 2-Byte Fixed Header.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fixed Header} &= \text{0x32} \;\; \text{0x1B} \\
&= [0011 \; 0010] \;\; [0001 \; 1011]
\end{aligned}
$$

This **2-byte** fixed header is followed by the variable header and payload — a stark contrast to HTTP's hundreds of bytes, making MQTT vastly more efficient for low-bandwidth IoT networks like LoRaWAN or NB-IoT.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MQTT Publish–Subscribe Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sensor1 Publisher"] -- "PUBLISH topic=home/temp payload=25.4" --> B["MQTT Broker Mosquitto port 1883"]
    C["MobileApp Subscriber"] -- "SUBSCRIBE topic=home/temp QoS=1" --> B
    D["CloudDashboard Subscriber"] -- "SUBSCRIBE topic=home/# QoS=2" --> B
    B -- "Forward to all matching subscribers" --> C
    B -- "Forward to all matching subscribers" --> D
    B -- "PUBACK QoS=1 ack" --> A
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The `Sensor1` (Publisher) and the two Subscribers are **completely decoupled** — the sensor has no IP knowledge of the mobile app or the dashboard.
- The `Broker` performs **topic matching** (e.g., the wildcard `home/#` matches `home/temp`).
- The `PUBACK` flow on the publisher side shows QoS 1 acknowledgment.

### 4.2 HTTP RESTful IoT Publishing Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as IoT Device Sensor
    participant TLS as TLS Handshake Layer
    participant API as Cloud REST API
    participant DB as Cloud Database
    
    Dev->>TLS: TCP SYN + TLS ClientHello
    TLS-->>Dev: TLS Finished
    Dev->>API: POST /api/sensors/data HTTP 1.1
    Note over Dev,API: Headers: Content-Type JSON, Authorization Bearer JWT
    Note over Dev,API: Body: deviceId, temp, hum
    API->>API: Validate JWT and parse JSON
    API->>DB: INSERT INTO sensor_readings
    DB-->>API: Ack
    API-->>Dev: HTTP 201 Created
    Note over API,Dev: Body: status success, id rec_9f2a
    Dev->>TLS: TCP FIN close
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The sequence is **strictly synchronous**: the device blocks until the `201 Created` response.
- TLS handshake occurs *before* the first HTTP request, adding latency.
- The database write is part of the request lifecycle — failure returns `500 Internal Server Error`.

### 4.3 Comparative Protocol Stack for IoT Publishing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MQTTStack["MQTT Protocol Stack"]
        MQ1["Application Layer MQTT Broker Publisher Subscriber"]
        MQ2["Message Layer CONNECT PUBLISH SUBSCRIBE QoS 0 1 2"]
        MQ3["Transport Layer TCP Port 1883 or 8883 TLS"]
        MQ4["Network Layer IP"]
    end
    subgraph HTTPStack["HTTP Protocol Stack"]
        HT1["Application Layer REST API Client Server"]
        HT2["HTTP Methods GET POST PUT DELETE"]
        HT3["Transport Layer TCP Port 80 or 443 TLS"]
        HT4["Network Layer IP"]
    end
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Both protocols share the same lower layers (TCP/IP); their difference lies in the **Application Layer semantics**.
- MQTT's **Topic-based routing** replaces HTTP's **URL-based routing**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between MQTT and HTTP as IoT application layer protocols. Mention any three points.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Architecture:** MQTT follows a **Publish–Subscribe** model mediated by a central broker, while HTTP follows a **Request–Response** model between a client and a server.
2. **Header Overhead:** MQTT has a minimal **2-byte** fixed header, ideal for constrained networks; HTTP has a variable header that is typically several hundred bytes, making it bandwidth-inefficient for tiny IoT packets.
3. **Real-Time Push:** MQTT supports **server push** natively (the broker pushes new data to subscribers immediately), while HTTP requires the client to **poll** (repeatedly send `GET` requests) to receive updates.
*(Valid third point: QoS guarantees, energy efficiency, or stateless vs stateful session.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
*Explain the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **QoS 0 — At Most Once:** The message is delivered once with no acknowledgment. It is a fire-and-forget mechanism with the lowest overhead; suitable for non-critical data like periodic environmental readings.
2. **QoS 1 — At Least Once:** A four-part handshake (`PUBLISH` → `PUBACK`) guarantees the message arrives, but duplicates are possible; suitable for most IoT telemetry.
3. **QoS 2 — Exactly Once:** A four-step handshake (`PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP`) ensures the message is delivered exactly once; used for mission-critical data such as billing or medical alerts.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — MQTT Focused**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MQTT Publish–Subscribe architecture**. Describe the roles of **Publisher, Subscriber, Broker, and Topic**. Also explain the **Topic Wildcards** `+` and `#` with an example. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain all **three Quality of Service (QoS) levels** in MQTT with their respective control packet exchange diagrams. Discuss a real-world scenario where each QoS level is appropriate. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MQTT Publish–Subscribe Architecture (7 Marks):**

*Architecture Diagram:*
```
[Sensor-Publisher] --PUBLISH home/room1/temp--> [MQTT BROKER] --Forward--> [Mobile App Subscriber]
                                            |
                                            +--Forward--> [Cloud Dashboard Subscriber]
```

*Component Roles:*
- **Publisher:** The IoT device (e.g., temperature sensor) that originates data. It sends a `PUBLISH` packet to the broker with a topic name and payload. The publisher has *no knowledge* of subscribers.
- **Subscriber:** The consumer of data (e.g., a mobile app, a cloud dashboard, an actuator). It sends a `SUBSCRIBE` packet to the broker to express interest in one or more topics.
- **Broker:** The central server (e.g., **Mosquitto**, **HiveMQ**, **EMQX**) that receives all messages, filters them by topic, and dispatches them to all matching subscribers. It runs on port **1883** (or 8883 for TLS).
- **Topic:** A UTF-8 string (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`) that acts as a routing key, organized hierarchically with `/` as a separator, similar to a file path.

*Topic Wildcards:*
- **`+` (Single-Level Wildcard):** Matches *exactly one* topic level. Example: Subscriber on `home/+/temperature` will receive data from `home/room1/temperature` and `home/room2/temperature` but **not** from `home/room1/floor1/temperature`.
- **`#` (Multi-Level Wildcard):** Matches *all remaining levels* and must be the **last character** in the topic filter. Example: Subscriber on `home/#` will receive every message whose topic begins with `home/`, including deeply nested ones like `home/room1/floor2/ac/temp`.

---

**(b) Three QoS Levels (7 Marks):**

*QoS 0 — At Most Once:*
```
Publisher --PUBLISH--> Subscriber  (No ACK)
```
A single `PUBLISH` packet is sent. No acknowledgment is expected. The message may be lost if the network fails. **Use case:** Frequent, non-critical environmental telemetry (e.g., air quality sampled every 10 seconds where losing one sample is acceptable).

*QoS 1 — At Least Once:*
```
Publisher --PUBLISH--> Subscriber
Subscriber --PUBACK--> Publisher
```
The publisher waits for a `PUBACK`. If not received within a timeout, it retransmits, so duplicates are possible. **Use case:** Smart home temperature control where duplicates can be filtered by the actuator logic.

*QoS 2 — Exactly Once:*
```
Publisher --PUBLISH--> Subscriber
Subscriber --PUBREC--> Publisher
Publisher --PUBREL--> Subscriber
Subscriber --PUBCOMP--> Publisher
```
A four-step handshake guarantees the message is delivered *exactly once*, eliminating both loss and duplication. **Use case:** Medical IoT device publishing a single critical alarm; financial transaction IoT; SCADA industrial commands.

*Engineering Trade-off:* Higher QoS means **more network round-trips**, **higher latency**, and **more battery consumption**, so it must be chosen carefully per data type.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — HTTP Focused (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain how **HTTP REST APIs** are used to publish IoT sensor data to the cloud. Illustrate with a sample `POST` request including headers and JSON body for a temperature sensor reading of 28.6 °C. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Compare **MQTT and HTTP** for IoT publishing across **eight** distinct parameters. Justify which protocol is more suitable for (i) a battery-powered soil moisture sensor in a remote farm, and (ii) a smart refrigerator uploading inventory to AWS. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HTTP REST API for IoT Publishing (7 Marks):**

REST (Representational State Transfer) treats every sensor as a **resource** identified by a URL. To publish a new sensor reading, the IoT device sends an HTTP `POST` request to the cloud's REST endpoint.

*Sample `POST` Request for Temperature = 28.6 °C:*
```
POST /api/v1/sensors/temperature HTTP/1.1
Host: iot-cloud.example.com
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 58
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...
Connection: close

{"deviceId":"TEMP-042","timestamp":1718905678,"value":28.6,"unit":"C"}
```

*Explanation of Key Components:*
- **Request Line:** `POST` creates a new resource; `/api/v1/sensors/temperature` is the endpoint; `HTTP/1.1` is the protocol version.
- **Host Header:** Mandatory in HTTP/1.1 to support virtual hosting.
- **Content-Type:** `application/json` tells the server to parse the body as JSON.
- **Content-Length:** Exactly **58 bytes** — required so the server knows when the body ends.
- **Authorization:** A JWT Bearer token for OAuth 2.0 device authentication.
- **Body:** The JSON payload containing the sensor reading, structured with a `deviceId`, UNIX timestamp, value, and unit.

*Typical Success Response:*
```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created
Content-Type: application/json
{"status":"success","recordId":"rec_7b3c"}
```

*Security Layers:* HTTPS (TLS) encrypts the channel; OAuth 2.0 / API keys authenticate the device; rate limiting protects the API from flooding.

---

**(b) MQTT vs HTTP — 8-Parameter Comparison (7 Marks):**

| # | Parameter | MQTT | HTTP |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Architecture | Publish–Subscribe (Broker) | Request–Response (Client–Server) |
| 2 | Header Overhead | **2 bytes** (fixed) | **~200–800 bytes** (variable) |
| 3 | Power Consumption | **Low** (long-lived TCP) | **High** (frequent handshakes) |
| 4 | Real-Time Push | Native (broker pushes) | Requires client polling |
| 5 | Reliability | 3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2) | TCP only; app-layer retries |
| 6 | Scalability (1-to-many) | Excellent (broker fan-out) | Poor (each client must call API) |
| 7 | Statelessness | Stateful (sessions, LWT) | Stateless (each request independent) |
| 8 | Typical Use | Telemetry, constrained devices | Cloud APIs, web dashboards |

*Justification for Specific Scenarios:*

**(i) Battery-Powered Soil Moisture Sensor in a Remote Farm:** **MQTT is the correct choice.** Justification: The sensor runs on a small battery, perhaps with a solar trickle charge, on a cellular NB-IoT or LoRaWAN network. MQTT's **2-byte header** minimizes data costs on metered cellular links, its **low power consumption** extends battery life, and the **broker model** allows the farmer's mobile app and irrigation controller to both receive data without the sensor needing to know them. QoS 1 is sufficient for soil moisture.

**(ii) Smart Refrigerator Uploading Inventory to AWS:** **HTTP (HTTPS REST) is the correct choice.** Justification: The refrigerator is mains-powered, always connected to home Wi-Fi, and uploads data sporadically (e.g., when the door closes and items are added/removed). It integrates natively with **AWS IoT Core, Google Cloud IoT, or Azure IoT Hub** REST endpoints. HTTP's statelessness is ideal for event-driven uploads, and HTTPS provides strong authentication via OAuth 2.0, which is well-supported by cloud platforms. The high header overhead is irrelevant on broadband.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Port Numbers:** Students often write **1884** instead of **1883** for MQTT. Memorize: MQTT = **1883**, MQTT TLS = **8883**; HTTP = **80**, HTTPS = **443**.
> 2. **Swapping QoS Handshakes:** QoS 2 is a **4-step** handshake (`PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP`), NOT 3-step. A common error is omitting `PUBREL`.
> 3. **Missing Content-Length in HTTP:** Forgetting `Content-Length` in HTTP `POST` requests is a frequent mark-deduction point; the server cannot parse the body without it.
> 4. **Saying "HTTP is More Secure":** Both protocols can be equally secure — MQTT over TLS (port 8883) and HTTPS. Security depends on implementation, not the protocol itself.
> 5. **Wildcard Misuse:** Writing `home#` instead of `home/#` — the `/` separator before `#` is **mandatory**.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **MQTT** = **Publish/Subscribe**, broker-mediated, **2-byte** header, port **1883/8883**, ISO/IEC **20922**.
- ✅ **HTTP** = **Request/Response**, client-server, large headers, port **80/443**, RFC **9110**.
- ✅ MQTT components: **Publisher**, **Subscriber**, **Broker**, **Topic** (with `+` single-level and `#` multi-level wildcards).
- ✅ MQTT QoS 0 = fire-and-forget; QoS 1 = `PUBLISH`+`PUBACK`; QoS 2 = 4-step handshake (exactly once).
- ✅ MQTT Keep-Alive timeout = **1.5 × KeepAlive interval**; client must send `PINGREQ` to stay connected.
- ✅ HTTP methods for IoT publishing: **`POST`** (create new) and **`PUT`** (update existing); `GET` is read-only.
- ✅ HTTP request requires `Content-Type` and `Content-Length` headers for any `POST`/`PUT` with a body.
- ✅ **MQTT** = battery-powered, constrained, real-time, many-to-many (e.g., sensor networks).
- ✅ **HTTP** = mains-powered, broadband, event-driven, cloud REST APIs (e.g., AWS IoT, smart appliances).
- ✅ **Last Will and Testament (LWT):** A pre-registered message the broker sends on behalf of a suddenly disconnected client — a unique MQTT feature for fault detection.
- ✅ **Retain Flag:** If set to 1 in a `PUBLISH` packet, the broker stores the last message and immediately delivers it to *new* subscribers — useful for "current state" data like device status.
- ✅ Hybrid systems (e.g., **AWS IoT Core**, **Azure IoT Hub**) support **both** MQTT and HTTPS, allowing the engineer to choose per device.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Web Pages for Your Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST755 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Creating Web Pages for Your Devices # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Web Pages for IoT Devices** refers to the design, development, and deployment of HTML/CSS/JavaScript-based graphical user interfaces hosted on embedded web servers (or companion gateway servers) that allow users to **monitor sensor data**, **control actuators**, and **configure device parameters** remotely through standard web browsers using HTTP/HTTPS protocols. In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic falls under the **Application Layer of the IoT Protocol Stack** and is a direct implementation of the **"Network-Application Convergence"** outcome specified in Module 4. The web page acts as the **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** for resource-constrained edge devices. ### Key Entities Involved - **Embedded Web Server**: A lightweight HTTP server (e.g., `WebServer.h` for ESP8266/ESP32, Flask for Raspberry Pi) that runs on the device itself. - **HTML Document**: The structured content layer (``, ``, ``). - **CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)**: The presentation layer that styles gauges, indicators, and control panels. - **JavaScript / AJAX / Fetch API**: The behavioral layer enabling asynchronous polling of sensor endpoints without page reload. - **REST Endpoints**: URI paths such as `/sensor/temperature` or `/led/on` exposed by the device. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Digital Notice Board + Remote Control"** > > Imagine your IoT device is a **security guard sitting in a locked room**. He cannot see you, and you cannot see him. To communicate, you pass **chits of paper** (HTTP requests) under the door. The guard writes back **status reports** (HTTP responses with sensor data) and also **executes commands** you write on the chit (e.g., "Turn on the light"). > > The **web page** is your *pre-printed chit pad*: it has neatly formatted forms, buttons, and read-out areas. The browser is the *chit-passing messenger*. The embedded web server is the *guard who reads the chit and replies*. You do not need a new messenger for every chit — one persistent messenger (TCP connection) handles it all. --- ## 1.3 Why "Web Pages" and Not Native Apps? | Feature | Native Mobile App | IoT Web Page (Browser) | |---|---|---| | Installation | Required | **Zero install** — open URL | | Cross-Platform | Per-OS build | **Universal** — any browser | | Update Path | App Store push | **Server-side refresh** | | Footprint on Device | Large | **KB-scale** (HTML+CSS+JS) | | Hardware Access | Via SDK/Plugins | **Via REST/WebSocket APIs** | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Highlight: For low-power IoT nodes (e.g., ESP8266 with only **80 KB RAM** and **4 MB flash**), the web page is typically stored in **PROGMEM (Program Memory / Flash)** as a C-string and served byte-by-byte via `server.send()`. This is the "Single-Page Static Dashboard" pattern. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Layers of an IoT Web Page The creation of a web page for an IoT device follows a strict three-layer architecture. The KTU 2024 module outcomes expect you to **label each layer and explain its role**. ### Layer 1 — The Content Layer (HTML5) - Defines semantic structure: `
`, `
`, `
`. - Hosts data placeholders such as `--` which JavaScript later populates. - KTU-favoured elements: `` for live charts, `` for gauges, `` for completion bars. ### Layer 2 — The Presentation Layer (CSS3) - Implements **responsive design** using media queries (`@media (max-width: 600px)`). - Uses **CSS Grid** and **Flexbox** for adaptive dashboard layouts. - Handles dark-mode toggles and color-coded status indicators (green = nominal, red = alert). ### Layer 3 — The Behaviour Layer (JavaScript) - **AJAX Polling**: Repeatedly calls the device's REST endpoint at fixed intervals. - **Fetch API** (modern, Promise-based replacement for XMLHttpRequest). - **WebSocket** for full-duplex, low-latency push notifications. - **Chart.js / D3.js** libraries for real-time data visualization. --- ## 2.2 The Request-Response Lifecycle (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) Every interaction between the browser and the IoT device follows the HTTP transaction cycle. The KTU 2024 examiner expects the student to identify the **HTTP verbs**, **status codes**, and **header fields**. ### Step-by-Step Transaction 1. **Browser Resolves Hostname** → DNS query maps `device.local` to IP (often via **mDNS** in IoT). 2. **TCP Three-Way Handshake** → SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK establishes a connection on **port 80** (HTTP) or **port 443** (HTTPS). 3. **Browser Sends HTTP Request** → Method (`GET`, `POST`), URI path, headers, optional body. 4. **Embedded Web Server Parses Request** → Routes to handler function. 5. **Server Sends HTTP Response** → Status line, headers, body (HTML/JSON). 6. **Connection Management** → `Connection: keep-alive` reuses the TCP socket for subsequent AJAX polls. 7. **Browser Renders** → HTML parsed → CSS applied → JavaScript executed → DOM tree constructed. --- ##
