2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **definitive revision sheet** for derivations and numerical problems on this topic. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Typical Value | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Page Weight (transfer size) | $W = N_H \cdot s_H + N_C \cdot s_C + N_J \cdot s_J$ | Bytes / KB | Bandwidth budgeting over LoRa/Wi-Fi | | 2 | AJAX Polling Latency | $T_{poll} = 2 \cdot RTT + t_{render}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Determines "liveness" feel of dashboard | | 3 | WebSocket Overhead | $OH_{ws} = 2 \text{ to } 14 \text{ bytes/frame}$ | bytes | Efficient vs HTTP polling overhead | | 4 | HTTP Polling Bandwidth | $B = \dfrac{P \cdot (S_{req} + S_{resp})}{T_{poll}}$ | bytes/sec | For sensor sampling design | | 5 | PROGMEM Capacity | $C_{flash} = 4 \text{ MB (ESP32)}$ | bytes | Max static page size on-device | | 6 | mDNS Cache Validity | $T_{TTL} = 60 \text{ s (default)}$ | seconds | Service discovery delay | | 7 | Status-Code Class | $1xx$ Info $\vert$ $2xx$ Success $\vert$ $3xx$ Redirect $\vert$ $4xx$ Client Err $\vert$ $5xx$ Server Err | — | Debugging response semantics | | 8 | TCP Keep-Alive | $t_{keep} \in [1, 60]$ s | seconds | Saves radio energy on battery nodes | **Symbol Legend** (vert-bar safe notation used throughout): - $N_H$ = number of HTML elements $\vert$ $s_H$ = average HTML size per element - $N_C$ = number of CSS rules $\vert$ $s_C$ = average CSS rule size - $N_J$ = number of JS scripts $\vert$ $s_J$ = average script size - $P$ = number of poll cycles $\vert$ $RTT$ = round-trip time - $S_{req}$ = request size $\vert$ $S_{resp}$ = response size --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In **Smart Agriculture**, an ESP32 in a polyhouse serves a `farm.local` page that displays soil moisture, ambient humidity, and valve status. The farmer opens the page on a 4G phone, taps a button, and the irrigation solenoid turns on — all through **HTTP POST** without installing any app. This is the **lowest-friction deployment pattern** and the one most often assessed in KTU ESE questions. In **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**, a Raspberry Pi gateway aggregates Modbus data from PLCs and serves a **real-time SCADA-style web dashboard** using Node-RED, Grafana, or a custom Flask + Chart.js stack. The same web page is consumable by an operator on a desktop, a supervisor on a tablet, and a maintenance engineer on a phone — **write once, render anywhere**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal AJAX Polling Interval **Problem Setup**: An ESP32-based weather node serves a JSON endpoint `/api/data` of size $S_{resp} = 256$ bytes. The browser sends GET requests of size $S_{req} = 128$ bytes. The measured RTT over the local Wi-Fi is $RTT = 40$ ms, and the browser's render time per update is $t_{render} = 60$ ms. The cellular backhaul (when remote) is capped at $B_{max} = 8$ KB/s. **Find the maximum sustainable polling interval.** ### Step 1: Total Latency Per Poll The latency experienced by the user between successive data points is the sum of network round-trip and rendering. $$ T_{poll} = 2 \cdot RTT + t_{render} $$ Substituting the given values: $$ T_{poll} = 2 \cdot 40 + 60 $$ $$ T_{poll} = 80 + 60 = 140 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 2: Per-Second Bandwidth Cost If the browser polls once every interval $I$ seconds, the number of polls per second is $\dfrac{1}{I}$. The bandwidth consumed per second is therefore: $$ B = \dfrac{S_{req} + S_{resp}}{I} $$ Plugging in the per-poll payload: $$ B = \dfrac{128 + 256}{I} = \dfrac{384}{I} \text{ bytes/s} $$ ### Step 3: Constraint From Backhaul Capacity We must enforce $B \leq B_{max}$, which yields: $$ \dfrac{384}{I} \leq 8192 \text{ bytes/s} $$ ### Step 4: Solve for the Minimum Interval $$ I \geq \dfrac{384}{8192} $$ $$ I \geq 0.0469 \text{ s} $$ ### Step 5: Conclusion $$ I_{min} \approx 47 \text{ ms} $$ Any polling faster than **47 ms** would saturate the backhaul. The **recommended practical interval** is **1 second** — it leaves 95% of the link idle and gives a perceived "real-time" feel because the human eye cannot distinguish updates faster than ~100 ms. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Budget for an On-Device HTML Page **Problem Setup**: An ESP8266 (4 MB flash) is to host a dashboard with 12 sensor cards. Each card is a `
` with ~150 bytes of HTML, a ~40-byte CSS class, and a ~30-byte JS update function. The library and a 6 KB chart engine are also embedded. **What fraction of flash is used?** ### Step 1: Compute HTML Footprint $$ N_H = 12, \quad s_H = 150 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ W_H = 12 \cdot 150 = 1800 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 2: Compute CSS Footprint $$ N_C = 12, \quad s_C = 40 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ W_C = 12 \cdot 40 = 480 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 3: Compute JS Footprint $$ N_J = 12, \quad s_J = 30 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ W_J = 12 \cdot 30 = 360 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 4: Add Library and Chart Engine $$ W_L = 6 \text{ KB} = 6144 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 5: Total Page Weight (Apply Master Formula) $$ W = W_H + W_C + W_J + W_L $$ $$ W = 1800 + 480 + 360 + 6144 $$ $$ W = 8784 \text{ bytes} \approx 8.58 \text{ KB} $$ ### Step 6: Express as a Fraction of Flash $$ f = \dfrac{W}{C_{flash}} = \dfrac{8784}{4 \cdot 1024 \cdot 1024} $$ $$ f = \dfrac{8784}{4194304} \approx 0.00209 $$ $$ f \approx 0.21\% $$ ### Step 7: Interpretation The dashboard consumes only **0.21%** of the available flash. This is the **engineering insight** the KTU 2024 examiner tests — you can comfortably host rich dashboards on chips that cost under ₹200. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Communication Pattern > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Compare HTTP polling, long-polling, server-sent events, and WebSockets for IoT dashboards."* The matrix below is the model answer. | Criterion | HTTP Polling | Long Polling | Server-Sent Events | WebSocket | |---|---|---|---|---| | Direction | Client→Server | Client→Server (held) | Server→Client | Full-duplex | | Latency | $T_{poll}$ | Sub-second | <100 ms | <50 ms | | Bandwidth Overhead | High (headers every poll) | Medium | Low | Very Low | | Browser Support | Universal | Universal | HTML5+ | HTML5+ | | Battery Impact | High (radio wakeups) | Medium | Low | Lowest | | KTU Use-Case | Temperature logger | Notification feed | Live log tail | Real-time control | | Complexity | Trivial | Moderate | Moderate | High | | Typical Frame Size | 200–800 B | 200–800 B | 50–200 B | 2–14 B overhead | --- ## 3.4 Mini Code-Snippet Blueprint (ESP32 + REST + AJAX) > The following is a **read-only, illustrative blueprint** of how the three layers connect. It is **not** to be memorised line-by-line; it is provided to anchor the theoretical concepts above. ```cpp // Server side: ESP32 serves the dashboard and a JSON endpoint server.on("/", HTTP_GET, []() { server.send(200, "text/html", DASHBOARD_HTML); // served from PROGMEM }); server.on("/api/sensor", HTTP_GET, []() { String json = "{\"temp\":" + String(readTemperature()) + ",\"hum\":" + String(readHumidity()) + "}"; server.send(200, "application/json", json); }); ``` ```html ``` ```css /* Presentation: a single responsive card */ .card { padding: 1rem; border-radius: 12px; background: #0f172a; color: #e2e8f0; } @media (max-width: 600px) { .card { font-size: 0.9rem; } } ``` # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of an IoT Web Dashboard The following Mermaid block renders the **complete functional architecture** of a web page hosted on an IoT device, mapping every interaction between the user, browser, network, embedded server, sensors, and actuators. This block-diagram style is the **block-level functional architecture fallback** prescribed for topics that cannot be drawn physically. ```mermaid flowchart TB user[End User] -->|tap click| browser[Web Browser on Phone or Laptop] browser -->|HTTP GET request| dns[DNS or mDNS Resolver] dns -->|resolved IP| browser browser -->|TCP SYN on port 80| device[IoT Device with Embedded Web Server] device -->|SYN ACK| browser browser -->|HTTP GET slash| server[Web Server Handler] server -->|read PROGMEM| html[HTML CSS JS Asset in Flash] server -->|HTTP 200 response| browser browser -->|parse and render| dom[DOM Tree Constructed] dom -->|start interval timer| js[JavaScript AJAX Loop] js -->|GET slash api sensor| device device -->|read ADC or I2C| sensor1[Sensor: Temperature] device -->|read ADC or I2C| sensor2[Sensor: Humidity] sensor1 -->|analog value| device sensor2 -->|digital value| device device -->|JSON response| browser browser -->|innerText update| dom dom -->|user sees refresh| user user -->|tap button LED on| browser browser -->|POST slash led on| device device -->|GPIO HIGH| actuator[Actuator: LED or Relay] actuator -->|physical action| world[Real World Effect] ``` --- ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — AJAX Polling Lifecycle This sequence diagram captures the **time-ordered message flow** between the browser, the embedded web server, and the sensor stack. It is the most common diagram KTU 2024 questions on "how data reaches the web page" expect. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant U as User Browser participant S as Embedded Web Server participant HW as Sensor Hardware U->>S: GET / (index page) S-->>U: 200 OK (HTML + CSS + JS bundle) Note over U: Browser parses DOM and starts setInterval(1000 ms) loop Every 1 second U->>S: GET /api/sensor S->>HW: readTemperature() HW-->>S: 27.4 deg C S-->>U: 200 OK (application/json) Note over U: innerText updated in card div end U->>S: POST /led/on S->>HW: digitalWrite(pin, HIGH) HW-->>S: ACK S-->>U: 200 OK (text/plain = LED ON) ``` --- ## 4.3 State Diagram — Web Page Lifecycle on the Device The following Mermaid state diagram tracks the **page-asset lifecycle** from creation through on-device storage to client-side execution. It maps directly to the KTU 2024 module outcomes on **storage and delivery**. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Authored: developer writes HTML CSS JS Authored --> Compressed: minify and gzip Compressed --> InFlash: store in PROGMEM or LittleFS InFlash --> Requested: browser sends GET slash Requested --> Parsed: HTTP 200 sent byte by byte Parsed --> Rendered: browser builds DOM and CSSOM Rendered --> Live: JavaScript AJAX loop starts Live --> Live: setInterval polls every 1 s Live --> Updated: sensor data injected into spans Updated --> Live: continues until tab closed Live --> [*]: connection close ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Layered Stack of an IoT Web Page This block diagram shows the **three layers** (Content, Presentation, Behaviour) and the **two transport options** (HTTP polling, WebSocket) the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to compare. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Application Dashboard] --> B[HTML5 Content Layer] A --> C[CSS3 Presentation Layer] A --> D[JavaScript Behaviour Layer] D --> E{Transport Choice} E -->|polling every 1 s| F[Fetch API to REST Endpoint] E -->|persistent socket| G[WebSocket ws colon slash slash] F --> H[Device HTTP Handler] G --> H H --> I[Sensor and Actuator Drivers] I --> J[Physical World] J --> I I --> H H --> F H --> G ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B, Q1a]** *Explain the role of an embedded web server in an IoT device. Why is it preferred over a native mobile application for low-cost deployments?* **Model Answer (3 marks)**: - **Definition (1 mark)**: An embedded web server is a lightweight HTTP server program (e.g., `WebServer.h`, Flask, Node-RED) that runs directly on the IoT device or its companion gateway. It listens on **port 80 (HTTP)** or **port 443 (HTTPS)** and serves HTML/CSS/JS assets to any browser on the same network. - **Resource Footprint (1 mark)**: A minimal Arduino-style server occupies only **~10 KB flash and ~2 KB RAM**, whereas a native Android APK starts at **~5 MB**. For ESP8266-class devices with **4 MB flash and 80 KB RAM**, a web server is the *only* feasible UI option. - **Zero-Install Advantage (1 mark)**: Users open a URL — no App Store gatekeeping, no per-OS rebuild, no forced updates. This is the central reason web pages are the **default KTU 2024 deployment pattern** for IoT. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Q1b]** *Differentiate between HTTP polling, long-polling, and WebSockets. State one IoT use-case for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks)**: - **HTTP Polling (1 mark)**: The browser sends a fresh GET request every fixed interval. **Use-case**: a temperature logger dashboard refreshing once per minute — simplicity outweighs overhead. - **Long-Polling (1 mark)**: The browser opens a request that the server *holds open* until new data is available, then replies. **Use-case**: a fire-alarm page that must display an alert within 1 second of detection. - **WebSocket (1 mark)**: A single upgraded TCP socket supports full-duplex, frame-based messaging with **2–14 bytes of overhead per frame**. **Use-case**: a remote-control console for a robotic arm where joystick movements must arrive with <50 ms latency. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — Full-Mark Question **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Slot A, Module 4, Q8a]** *Design a web-based dashboard for an ESP32-based **Smart Irrigation System** that monitors soil moisture (%), ambient temperature (°C), and controls a solenoid valve. Your answer must include:* *(a) [7 marks] The system architecture, the HTML structure of the dashboard, and the CSS styling strategy for a responsive mobile-first layout.* *(b) [7 marks] The JavaScript AJAX polling logic, the corresponding REST endpoints on the device, and an analysis of the polling bandwidth consumption.* #### Model Solution **(a) Architecture and Front-End [7 marks]** **System Architecture (2 marks)**: The system has three tiers: **(i) Perception tier** — capacitive soil-moisture sensor on ADC pin 34, DHT22 on GPIO 4, solenoid driver (MOSFET + flyback diode) on GPIO 26; **(ii) Network tier** — ESP32 hosts a softAP and an embedded web server on port 80; **(iii) Application tier** — any browser on the LAN opens `http://192.168.4.1/` to view the dashboard. **HTML Structure (3 marks)**: A semantic skeleton using `
`, a `
` with three `
` blocks for moisture, temperature, and valve, and a `
` for last-refresh timestamp. Each card contains a labelled ``, a `` for visual level, and a `
